name: Trey Trainor for Texas
subject: Join Trey's Troops – Be Part of the Winning Team in TX-21!
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TREY TRAINOR FOR TEXAS
body:
Trey's Troops is launching now—your chance to help secure victory on March 3rd. Join today!

| | | | | | | [View this email in your browser](https://mailchi.mp/e6129a074ebb/join-treys-troops?e=4d3fe76c62)
---
[|
---](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=10f6eb97fe&e=4d3fe76c62)
| | | | | | Peter,
Today, I'm excited to officially announce the launch of **Trey's Troops** —our dedicated grassroots army of supporters and volunteers who are stepping up to make sure we win the TX-21 Republican primary on March 3rd and send a proven conservative fighter to Congress.
---
[|
---](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=3fff42dc04&e=4d3fe76c62)
| |
Why "Trey's Troops"? Because that's exactly what you are: patriots ready to fight for limited government, election integrity, secure borders, free markets, and America First principles. We've already built an incredible team of phone bankers, door knockers, and organizers who are making real, personal contact with voters every day—and the momentum is building fast.
Now it's your turn to join the ranks.
With early voting approaching and the race heating up, every pair of hands, every voice, and every bit of energy matters. Whether you can make calls from home, knock doors in your neighborhood, host a sign wave, share posts on social media, or donate to fuel the effort—**there's a role for you in**[**Trey's Troops**](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=20fbb992f0&e=4d3fe76c62).
Here's how to get started today:
* **Sign up** —visit our [**volunteer page**](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=3c8c1cfcde&e=4d3fe76c62) to sign up and tell us how you'd like to help
* Text "**TROOPS** " to [(210) 245-7756](tel:2102457756) (or reply to this email) for quick next steps
* [**Donate**](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=f8ddf32726&e=4d3fe76c62)**to fuel the campaign** —ads, mailers, phones, and ground game all require resources. Even $25, $50, or $100 makes a huge difference when multiplied by thousands of patriots. If you can't give time right now, your financial support is a powerful way to equip the troops and help us win!

We've set big goals and with supporters like you turning into active Troops, we'll crush them and win this race.
I am truly humbled by the outpouring of support I've received so far. Together, we will defend Texas values, hold Washington accountable, and deliver the conservative leadership TX-21 deserves—without apology.
Don't wait—**join Trey's Troops** right now and let's make history in CD-21!
For Texas and for freedom,
**Trey Trainor** Republican Candidate for Congress, TX-21
---
| [Support Trey](https://Trey4TX.us10.list-manage.com/track/click?u=c9a64ea8a402e6526a97d9207&id=4f1949b7b2&e=4d3fe76c62)
---
|

Join today! | | | | | | | | | | | | | Peter, Today, I'm excited to officially announce the launch of Trey's Troops —our dedicated grassroots army of supporters and volunteers who are stepping up to make sure we win the TX-21 Republican primary on March 3rd and send a proven conservative fighter to Congress. | | Why "Trey's Troops"? Because that's exactly what you are: patriots ready to fight for limited government, election integrity, secure borders, free markets, and America First principles. We've already built an incredible team of phone bankers, door knockers, and organizers who are making real, personal contact with voters every day—and the momentum is building fast. Now it's your turn to join the ranks. With early voting approaching and the race heating up, every pair of hands, every voice, and every bit of energy matters. Whether you can make calls from home, knock doors in your neighborhood, host a sign wave, share posts on social media, or donate to fuel the effort—there's a role for you in. Here's how to get started today: Sign up —visit our to sign up and tell us how you'd like to help Text "TROOPS " to tel:2102457756 (or reply to this email) for quick next steps fuel the campaign —ads, mailers, phones, and ground game all require resources. Even $25, $50, or $100 makes a huge difference when multiplied by thousands of patriots. If you can't give time right now, your financial support is a powerful way to equip the troops and help us win! We've set big goals and with supporters like you turning into active Troops, we'll crush them and win this race. I am truly humbled by the outpouring of support I've received so far. Together, we will defend Texas values, hold Washington accountable, and deliver the conservative leadership TX-21 deserves—without apology. Don't wait—join Trey's Troops right now and let's make history in CD-21! For Texas and for freedom, Trey Trainor Republican Candidate for Congress, TX-21 | | | | Early voting: February 17 – 27, 2026**Election Day: March 3, 2026 | | | | | | https:// | | | | | | PAID FOR BY TREY TRAINOR FOR TEXAS | | | | | Copyright (C) 2026 Trey Trainor for Texas. All rights reserved.

Colorado is one of the key states that Republicans and corporate interests will be watching this year.

[ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123620441/596039437/-1473932214?refcode=logo&amount=10&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTAvMS83MjY1OCIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJjNTdhOTBlOS00ZmY1LWYwMTEtOGQ0Yy0wMDIyNDgyZDI3OWIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=zNWRWVEVg11h1a7VFdnhKUmNPkMeMN_Rwf2fo5ncfiI=) Hi there, with the election year in full swing, we wanted to take a moment to catch you up to speed on where things stand for our campaign. **Colorado is one of the key states that Republicans and corporate interests will be watching this year.** They know that this seat was hard-won in 2020, and they’ve made it clear they’re prepared to spend big to flip this seat back to red and expand their Senate majority. **It’s also clear that Colorado is on the top of the president’s mind.** In the last month, he’s threatened our state repeatedly: announcing plans to dismantle NCAR, targeting child care, and vetoing clean water infrastructure for rural Coloradans. All because John and Coloradans refuse to bend to his will. **If the last month is any indication, Colorado is going to play a big role in the midterm elections, and will impact whether we take back control of the Senate or not.** That’s why our team is working hard to gather the resources and grassroots energy we need to protect this seat and keep John fighting for our families and communities in the Senate. **_[With that being said, we need your help. ---
Team, Today, on Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day, we pause to honor a leader whose commitment to justice, service, and equality continues to guide us today. Dr. King taught us that change requires action and care for one another. As he said, _"Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate; only love can do that._ " **That sentiment is just as important today as it was during the civil rights movement.** I know that this is an uncertain time for many Americans. Dr. King's legacy teaches us that even in difficult times, each of us has the power to make a difference through service, compassion, and standing up for what's right. Today is a reminder that each of us has a role to play. Service doesn't look the same for everyone. For some, it might mean volunteering or standing up for a neighbor. For others, it might mean simply choosing compassion when it would be easier to look away. Through these acts, big and small, Dr. King's dream lives on. As we honor Dr. King today, let's recommit ourselves to building a future full of equal opportunities and dignity for all. Thank you,
Lisa Blunt Rochester
* * *

If you would like to get less email, To unsubscribe, | | = Team, Today, on Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Day, we pause to honor a leader whose commitment to justice, service, and equality continues to guide us today. Dr. King taught us that change requires action and care for one another. As he said, "Darkness cannot drive out darkness; only light can do that. Hate cannot drive out hate; only love can do that. " That sentiment is just as important today as it was during the civil rights movement. I know that this is an uncertain time for many Americans. Dr. King's legacy teaches us that even in difficult times, each of us has the power to make a difference through service, compassion, and standing up for what's right. Today is a reminder that each of us has a role to play. Service doesn't look the same for everyone. For some, it might mean volunteering or standing up for a neighbor. name: Mark Kelly
subject: One year ago today
disclaimer_text: Paid For By Mark Kelly for Senate
body:
I’m sure you had some of the same fears I did. And over the last year, many of those fears came to pass. Our nation has been tested like no other time in our lives.
#

Folks, it's Mark.
Donald Trump took office on January 20th, 2025.
So, one year ago today, I’m sure you had some of the same fears I did. And over the last year, many of those fears came to pass. Our nation has been tested like no other time in our lives.
But still, we are here. It took courage, it took resilience, it took grit — but we are here.
We’re heading into another year that will test us. And like last year, there’s uncertainty ahead.
For me, though, I am proud. I continue to be proud of this country, our Constitution, and our people. I still believe in the best of America. I hope you do, too.
And together, I know we’ll make it to the other side of this.
Hold fast,
Mark

Peter, it’s Congresswoman Valerie Foushee – Today, on Martin Luther King Jr. Day, we honor the life and legacy of Dr. King not only by remembering his words, but by recommitting ourselves to the work he began: the ongoing fight for justice, equality, and opportunity for all. Dr. King helped change the course of history, and his legacy challenges each of us to do more than reflect, but to act. As we honor the sacrifices that brought our nation to this moment, we must also look forward, determined to build a future that lives up to his vision. We are living in a moment of deep political division. Yet as we continue our work for justice and equality, I hope we can hold fast to the words of Dr. King: “We must accept finite disappointment, but never lose infinite hope.” May we honor his legacy today, and every day, by choosing hope, justice, and progress for generations to come. Sincerely, Valerie Foushee
* * *

name: Phil Weiser
subject: Martin Luther King Jr.’s words today
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Phil Weiser for Colorado
body:
How we bend our world toward justice today.

[ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123622667/596046339/1239926708?refcode=ea.27736117-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ms=ea.27736117-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTAvMS84MTM2MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIyNzczNjExNy01NGY1LWYwMTEtOGQ0Yy0wMDIyNDgyZDI3OWIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=lHec8f3K1yit-iWBcRg4KxPKQxh5lY-y6kW53oualI0=&emci=cf6edd38-27f3-f011-8d4c-000d3a54b560&emdi=27736117-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ceid=51844520) Friends, it’s Phil. Over the course of this campaign, you may have already heard me share one particular Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. quote that is meaningful to me and I think of often: **_"Darkness cannot drive out darkness, only light can do that; and hate cannot drive out hate, only love can do that."_** No email is long enough to encompass the entirety of Dr. King’s leadership, vision, and impact. But I’d like to share a few more pieces of his wisdom today, especially as they relate to what drives this movement we’re building together. At the Ralph Carr building where I work, there is a powerful quote from Dr. King: **"Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere."** As I fight for a better Colorado, I will lead with the spirit that all of us need all of us. That includes fighting against lawlessness from the federal government, abusive practices from irresponsible corporations, and the dangers of climate change, to name just a few specific examples. Not long before he was murdered, Dr. King famously said, **"We shall overcome because the arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice."** My faith teaches that we are all called to action — to repair our world — and to do our part to make the arc bend. This change won’t occur unless we take action. And no person can make it happen alone. Thank you for being in this fight with us. | | [DONATE](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123622669/596046340/-246109695?refcode=ea.27736117-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ms=ea.27736117-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTAvMS84MTM2MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIyNzczNjExNy01NGY1LWYwMTEtOGQ0Yy0wMDIyNDgyZDI3OWIiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=lHec8f3K1yit-iWBcRg4KxPKQxh5lY-y6kW53oualI0=)
---
With gratitude and a commitment toward the continued work for justice and peace,
Phil
|

clean_body: How we bend our world toward justice today. Friends, it’s Phil. Over the course of this campaign, you may have already heard me share one particular Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. quote that is meaningful to me and I think of often: "Darkness cannot drive out darkness, only light can do that; and hate cannot drive out hate, only love can do that." No email is long enough to encompass the entirety of Dr. King’s leadership, vision, and impact. But I’d like to share a few more pieces of his wisdom today, especially as they relate to what drives this movement we’re building together. At the Ralph Carr building where I work, there is a powerful quote from Dr. King: "Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere." As I fight for a better Colorado, I will lead with the spirit that all of us need all of us. That includes fighting against lawlessness from the federal government, abusive practices from irresponsible corporations, and the dangers of climate change, to name just a few specific examples. Not long before he was murdered, Dr. King famously said, "We shall overcome because the arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice." My faith teaches that we are all called to action — to repair our world — and to do our part to make the arc bend. This change won’t occur unless we take action. And no person can make it happen alone. Thank you for being in this fight with us. | | = With gratitude and a commitment toward the continued work for justice and peace, Phil | Make sure this email goes to your inbox. name: Charlene Fernandez, Chair
subject: Martin Luther King’s legacy in Arizona
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY THE ARIZONA DEMOCRATIC PARTY
body:
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/azd-em-header [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/azd-em-header]
Team,

Today, as we honor Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s legacy, I'm thinking about what his example means for Arizona — and why the Arizona Democratic Party celebrates his values of nonviolent civic engagement, dignity, and opportunity for all every single day.

Arizona has a complicated history with this holiday. We were the last state in the nation to recognize Martin Luther King Jr. Day, and we're the only state that approved it by popular vote. That history matters, and it's worth remembering.

In 1986, Governor Bruce Babbitt established the holiday by executive order after the legislature failed to act. But in 1987, his successor, Evan Mecham, rescinded it as his first act in office. He called it "illegal" and eventually moved the observance to an unpaid Sunday.

What followed was a six-year political and economic conflict that put Arizona's values — and our economy — on the line. Major entertainers boycotted the state. In 1990, Arizona voters rejected two separate ballot measures to establish the holiday. The NFL responded by stripping Arizona of Super Bowl XXVII, costing our state an estimated $200 million.

Finally, in November 1992, Arizona voters approved the establishment of MLK Day. We celebrated our first official Martin Luther King Jr. Day on January 18, 1993 — more than six years after the holiday became federal law.

I share this history because Dr. King's example reminds us that the work to create a fairer, more just state and country is ongoing. It took years of organizing, economic pressure, and civic engagement to do what should have been done immediately: honor a man who gave his life fighting for equality, justice, and dignity for all Americans.

That fight continues today.

At the Arizona Democratic Party, we honor Dr. King's legacy by working each day to build a state where everyone has dignity and opportunity, regardless of where they come from, what they look like, or how much money they have.

We organize in all 15 counties because Dr. King taught us that justice isn't just for some communities, it's for all of them. We register voters because he believed in the power of civic participation to transform our democracy. We stand up against hatred and extremism because he showed us that silence in the face of injustice is complicity.

We defend voting rights, fight for affordable healthcare, protect workers' dignity, and build coalitions across lines of difference — because that's what Dr. King's vision demands of us.

Arizona was the last state to recognize this holiday. But we can be the first to live up to the values it represents: equality, justice, dignity, and opportunity for all.

Thank you for supporting all we do to build that Arizona — not just today, but every day.

In solidarity,

Charlene Fernandez
Chair, Arizona Democratic Party
Arizona Democratic Party
PO Box 36123
Phoenix, AZ 85067-6123
United States
info@azdem.org [info@azdem.org]

name: Jane Fonda
subject: What Dr. King taught me about courage
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Jane Fonda Climate PAC, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee
body:
And why his legacy matters more than ever right now.

| [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123622890/596047055/1328794144?refcode=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ms=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAxNi8xLzk2NzgyIiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjU1MWJkMGVhLTU0ZjUtZjAxMS04ZDRjLTAwMjI0ODJkMjc5YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ntkgyTUQY7T5Y9Ggf9vCkBX3sWAlsxiirqEvcw58fB4=&emci=3b92aba2-39f2-f011-8194-000d3a11f903&emdi=551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ceid=4823105)
---
**On Martin Luther King Jr. Day, I always reflect on what Dr. King taught us about courage.** Dr. King showed us that silence in the face of injustice is complicity. That's what drove me to become an activist and fight for something bigger than myself. He taught us that when a moment demands courage, we have to meet it — even when it's hard, even when it costs us something. **Right now, we are living in one of those moments.** Donald Trump is doing everything to attack our civil rights and most fundamental freedoms. He recently told _The New York Times_ that civil rights protections resulted in white people being “very badly treated.” In other words, the same laws that finally began to dismantle centuries of systemic racism — laws that Dr. King gave his life fighting for — are now being reframed as the “real discrimination”. This narrative is coming from a man who has dismantled diversity offices and halted enforcement of the Civil Rights Act. The same disregard for justice extends to every corner of this administration: An illegal war for oil in Venezuela. Environmental protections destroyed to enrich fossil fuel executives. ICE raids terrorizing communities across the country. It's deeply painful, and it's deliberately overwhelming. But I’m seeing something else, too. I see people refusing to look away. Neighbors organizing. Communities protecting one another. Thousands showing up to protest because they understand, as Dr. King said, _injustice anywhere, is a threat to justice everywhere._ I may be 88, but I have no plans of slowing down. At JanePAC, we are fighting to elect leaders with backbone — leaders who will protect their communities, stand up to bullies, and carry forward Dr. King’s legacy by confronting injustice rather than accommodating it. I refuse to watch everything he stood for be erased without doing something about it. His legacy demands that we challenge the status quo, speak out when it’s uncomfortable, and stand up for what’s right — especially now, when authoritarianism is knocking at our door. The fight for justice, whether it's civil rights, climate justice, or our democracy itself, has always been **_connected._** And it's never been more urgent. I’m grateful to be alive. I’m grateful to be in this work with you. In solidarity, Jane Fonda [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123622891/596047056/2105508926?refcode=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ms=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAxNi8xLzk2NzgyIiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjU1MWJkMGVhLTU0ZjUtZjAxMS04ZDRjLTAwMjI0ODJkMjc5YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ntkgyTUQY7T5Y9Ggf9vCkBX3sWAlsxiirqEvcw58fB4=) | [**DONATE**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123622892/596047057/2105508926?refcode=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ms=ea.551bd0ea-54f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAxNi8xLzk2NzgyIiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjU1MWJkMGVhLTU0ZjUtZjAxMS04ZDRjLTAwMjI0ODJkMjc5YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=ntkgyTUQY7T5Y9Ggf9vCkBX3sWAlsxiirqEvcw58fB4=)

clean_body: And why his legacy matters more than ever right now. | On Martin Luther King Jr. Day, I always reflect on what Dr. King taught us about courage. Dr. King showed us that silence in the face of injustice is complicity. That's what drove me to become an activist and fight for something bigger than myself. He taught us that when a moment demands courage, we have to meet it — even when it's hard, even when it costs us something. Right now, we are living in one of those moments. Donald Trump is doing everything to attack our civil rights and most fundamental freedoms. He recently told The New York Times that civil rights protections resulted in white people being “very badly treated.” In other words, the same laws that finally began to dismantle centuries of systemic racism — laws that Dr. King gave his life fighting for — are now being reframed as the “real discrimination”. This narrative is coming from a man who has dismantled diversity offices and halted enforcement of the Civil Rights Act. The same disregard for justice extends to every corner of this administration: An illegal war for oil in Venezuela. Environmental protections destroyed to enrich fossil fuel executives. ICE raids terrorizing communities across the country. It's deeply painful, and it's deliberately overwhelming. But I’m seeing something else, too. I see people refusing to look away. Neighbors organizing. Communities protecting one another. Thousands showing up to protest because they understand, as Dr. King said, injustice anywhere, is a threat to justice everywhere. I may be 88, but I have no plans of slowing down. At JanePAC, we are fighting to elect leaders with backbone — leaders who will protect their communities, stand up to bullies, and carry forward Dr. King’s legacy by confronting injustice rather than accommodating it. I refuse to watch everything he stood for be erased without doing something about it. His legacy demands that we challenge the status quo, speak out when it’s uncomfortable, and stand up for what’s right — especially now, when authoritarianism is knocking at our door. The fight for justice, whether it's civil rights, climate justice, or our democracy itself, has always been connected. And it's never been more urgent. I’m grateful to be alive. I’m grateful to be in this work with you. In solidarity, Jane Fonda = | = | = | = = Follow along: | | | | | ---|---|---|---|---|--- Paid for by Jane Fonda Climate PAC, not authorized by any candidate or candidate’s committee. Send donations via check to: Jane Fonda Climate PAC 600 Pennsylvania Ave SE #15180 Washington, DC 20003 United States

Fellow Patriot,

I’m going to be brutally honest with you.

Arizona is _ground zero._

If Democrats get even a little bit of power back in 2026, they’re going to turn our state into a political punching bag and a testing ground for their next wave of insanity. I chased fentanyl traffickers.

**Here’s what the Democrats want:**

They want open-border chaos to keep going.
They want the fentanyl pipeline wide open.
They want Americans to stay overwhelmed, unsafe, and ignored.

And if they flip the House? They will come after EVERY SINGLE Republican who dared challenge their socialist policies.

**They’ll use the gavel to impeach President Trump, block the America First agenda, and spend two years doing investigations and political theater while our border continues to collapse.**

clean_body: | Fellow Patriot, I’m going to be brutally honest with you. Arizona is ground zero. If Democrats get even a little bit of power back in 2026, they’re going to turn our state into a political punching bag and a testing ground for their next wave of insanity. And that starts with flipping seats like this one. They don’t hate me because of my “tone” or because I’m “too conservative.” They hate me because I’ve done the job they refuse to do: I fought cartels. I chased fentanyl traffickers. I watched families get ripped apart because politicians in D.C. turned the border into a joke. Here’s what the Democrats want: They want open-border chaos to keep going. They want the fentanyl pipeline wide open. They want Americans to stay overwhelmed, unsafe, and ignored. And if they flip the House? They will come after EVERY SINGLE Republican who dared challenge their socialist policies. They’ll use the gavel to impeach President Trump, block the America First agenda, and spend two years doing investigations and political theater while our border continues to collapse. Because the radical left is going to dump millions into Arizona—they already are. And I’m not backing down. I’m running to secure the border, crush the cartels, and finally put America First. But I need backup. - — Mark Lamb Join Mark Lamb on social media! | | PAID FOR BY MARK LAMB FOR CONGRESS Don't want to receive any more of our emails? We hate to see you go: .

name: LeftNet
subject: "The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice"
body:
Today is a reminder of Dr. Martin Luther King’s legacy and what we are still fighting for.
#
Today, we honor Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., but it is important to understand what this day means.
Dr. King did not just dream. He fought. He organized. He marched _for_ justice and _against_ poverty, racism, and war. King understood that real change does not come from waiting around, but from everyday people demanding it.
**MLK Day is not just a day of remembrance. It’s a call to action.**
Right now, working families are struggling to afford basic needs. Families are being torn apart by violence. Safety net programs are being cut due to corporate greed. The issues that MLK gave his life for are still here.
But here’s the truth: _MLK’s movement did not die with him_. It exists in every person who refuses to accept injustice and inequality. In those organizing their communities and in every act of resistance – big or small.
Today, we remember Dr. King by continuing his fight.
We honor him by standing up for economic justice for the working class, protecting the right to vote, and fighting for a country that works for **all of us.**
_“The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice,”_ and we are the ones who have to bend it.
In solidarity,
LeftNet

**LeftNet is an online political network fighting for progressive causes and candidates focused on social, racial, economic, and environmental justice.**
---
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is a critical way we communicate with grassroots supporters like you. However, if you would like to change your subscription preferences, here are some options:
* [Receive Fewer Emails](https://act.leftnet.org/signup/receive_fewer_emails/?t=1&akid=1465%2E2118727%2ErZnPzx)
* [Update Your Information](https://act.leftnet.org/signup/update_your_information/?t=2&akid=1465%2E2118727%2ErZnPzx)
* [Unsubscribe](https://act.leftnet.org/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?t=3&akid=1465%2E2118727%2ErZnPzx)

Email us: [team@leftnet.org](mailto:team@leftnet.org)

subject: Dr. King taught us that justice relies on truth.

Today we honor the life and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

| | [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123623119/596047750/-718111328?refcode=LOC_EM_NONFR_2026.01.19_MLK_B1_F1_S1_C1_AC_N-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTkvMS8xMDYwODgiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiZTA3NjBjMmUtNTZmNS1mMDExLThkNGMtMDAyMjQ4MmQyNzliIiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=BdLJVru7cYqSJTz8DNn_pqMD79U-R-61NKDWAgTWeIQ=&emci=dc0c2d0f-f8f2-f011-8d4c-000d3a54b560&emdi=e0760c2e-56f5-f011-8d4c-0022482d279b&ceid=626985)

Friend, today we honor the life and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Dr. King taught us that justice relies on truth. Injustice is a cancer that grows with silence and lies, but change starts when we face what's wrong and demand better. As a former broadcast journalist, I spent years doing exactly that — asking hard questions, exposing corruption, and holding the powerful accountable. Dr. King’s legacy is an inspiration to me. He forced the nation to confront uncomfortable truths, and he refused to let injustice go unseen or unchallenged. Today, when lies spread faster than facts, and those in power try to squash accountability, Dr. King’s legacy feels especially urgent. A free press, a strong democracy, and an informed public are not optional **— they are the foundation of true justice.** **On this day of reflection for Martin Luther King Jr., I’m reminded of our shared responsibility to protect the truth, defend our democracy, and keep fighting for dignity and equality for all.** Thank you for standing with me and for carrying Dr. King’s legacy forward. Together, Marlene Galán-Woods

name: Rashida Tlaib
subject: MLK Day of Service and the ongoing work toward racial justice
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Rashida Tlaib for Congress
body:
Today is MLK Day. We must continue working for liberation and racial justice. | | | [](https://click.actionnetwork.org/ss/c/u001.X6XUC7u8ppFLNtMO_55SZlNseb7gEYIOhm_EWnhzEOZKmTtnV3k2h4V2x_O3H7rdW2az5rgktBwzxQ8Ml9Wgkmv85qW-UsBonL4KRa0bcNqdICU6T3NBDdNIZG7w7wEWbm9zNH6L1VtNm7_Q9ctcVrYYGlVYCE-uMd25kmCKCe_WxPBSLcUk4uP-dHtFCRqk-BB8UFE9A2KLhQmF-IMhN_QLs3as0TxdMlsX5dWjSGPFjl6eUXjSYnUIAJhOSB397HpMIZl9JuY_IIAIgFqANfZfvmmD_P2WxmKrB_kTue1kJftSMKizlLB7fwvA2NB5SWVqyW5N3EvW2w7REpBE1r4IYAiDjtMvBBRvarnstZ8/4nf/w6tBJL5GS0azCyeq7us_2g/h0/h001.XymdBA-59jU9X7bae81_MokvbwcyVBZRDq27v_h38aE)
---
Friend, Today is MLK Day. To honor the powerful legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. and the civil rights movement, we must continue working for liberation and racial justice. Today let’s remember that transformative change doesn’t come from the government; it comes from everyday people pushing for our rights. People in Congress and the White House didn’t just wake up one day and decide to pass transformative policies like the _Civil Rights Act_. They were pushed by years of growing people power, which Black Americans built in their communities. People power works— _and_ it’s a continual effort. Movements have continued to win progress and shift our culture. We’ve built real power, but anti-Blackness was built into our country’s institutions. It’s still here, and now we’re experiencing a frightening racist backlash to our movements’ growing power—with a presidential administration that’s explicitly white nationalist and anti-Black. I promise to continue working alongside grassroots movements to push back against Trump’s racism _and_ to dismantle systemic racism, solve the Black maternal health crisis, strengthen our civil rights, close the racial wealth gap, invest in Black communities, deliver environmental justice, and transform our country’s approach to public safety so we center care rather than racist policing or criminalization. In order to understand and help change our country’s anti-Black present, we don’t have to look that far into our country’s past. For example, ICE’s activity has roots in slave patrols, as well as Jim Crow laws and the genocide of Native Americans. This country has never lived up to its ideals. But there have always been people here who work to (as Dr. King said) “make real the promises of democracy.” We stand on the shoulders of our movement ancestors, and we honor them by carrying on their legacy. So much change starts from people coming together in their communities to make things better. We’re seeing that happen right now. One way to come together to support each other is through community service. The Martin Luther King, Jr. Center for Nonviolent Social Change encourages people to participate in a day of service today. clean_body: Today is MLK Day. We must continue working for liberation and racial justice. | | | Friend, Today is MLK Day. To honor the powerful legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. and the civil rights movement, we must continue working for liberation and racial justice. Today let’s remember that transformative change doesn’t come from the government; it comes from everyday people pushing for our rights. People in Congress and the White House didn’t just wake up one day and decide to pass transformative policies like the Civil Rights Act. They were pushed by years of growing people power, which Black Americans built in their communities. People power works— and it’s a continual effort. Movements have continued to win progress and shift our culture. We’ve built real power, but anti-Blackness was built into our country’s institutions. It’s still here, and now we’re experiencing a frightening racist backlash to our movements’ growing power—with a presidential administration that’s explicitly white nationalist and anti-Black. I promise to continue working alongside grassroots movements to push back against Trump’s racism and to dismantle systemic racism, solve the Black maternal health crisis, strengthen our civil rights, close the racial wealth gap, invest in Black communities, deliver environmental justice, and transform our country’s approach to public safety so we center care rather than racist policing or criminalization. In order to understand and help change our country’s anti-Black present, we don’t have to look that far into our country’s past. For example, ICE’s activity has roots in slave patrols, as well as Jim Crow laws and the genocide of Native Americans. This country has never lived up to its ideals. But there have always been people here who work to (as Dr. King said) “make real the promises of democracy.” We stand on the shoulders of our movement ancestors, and we honor them by carrying on their legacy. So much change starts from people coming together in their communities to make things better. We’re seeing that happen right now. One way to come together to support each other is through community service. The Martin Luther King, Jr. Center for Nonviolent Social Change encourages people to participate in a day of service today. —today and beyond. Thank you. Together we will continue pushing for a fully realized multi-racial democracy, with equity and dignity for all. Onward together in solidarity, Rashida Rashida Tlaib for Congress PO Box 32777 Detroit, MI 48232 | Paid for by Rashida Tlaib for Congress | | Sent via . To update your email address, change your name or address, or to stop receiving emails from Rashida Tlaib for Congress, please .

name: Jocelyn Benson
subject: Honoring Dr. King, today and every day
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY JOCELYN BENSON FOR GOVERNOR
body:
# [](https://act.jocelynbenson.com/go/203?t=1&akid=1386%2E181023%2EPjL7nk)
_“However difficult the moment, however frustrating the hour, it will not be long, because truth crushed to earth will rise again. How long? Not long, because no lie can live forever.”_
In the darkest days following the 2020 Presidential election, when President Trump used lies and threats in an attempt to overturn the results of a legitimate presidential election because he lost, I read the speech Dr. King delivered at the Alabama state capitol following the Selma-Montgomery voting rights march in 1965. These words — reminding us that truth always will rise and that no lie can live forever — fueled me as we worked every day to battle the false conspiracy theories and frivolous court cases designed to dampen our faith in democracy and our votes.
I started my career working for the Southern Poverty Law Center in Montgomery, Alabama. As I investigated, undercover, the hateful activity of far-right extremists groups, I saw firsthand how we all have a responsibility to carry on the spirit and work of Dr. King and so many others who fought and bled and marched to demand our country live up to the promises of our Constitution. A world where equality reigns and justice prevails.
And now here we are, over 60 years since Dr. King delivered those words, seeing our country march backwards. Medicaid gutted. Access to health care unaffordable for far too many. Stagnant wages while the cost of everything — from medicine to food to housing — escalates.
I am running for governor of Michigan in 2026 because the work of those who fought and bled and marched for a better world is unfinished. And it’s up to us, now, to carry their baton forward. Not just for those who came before us, but for the young boys and girls who will inherit what we leave behind.
**[I'll never give up on this fight to build a state where every person has a fair shot to live, work, vote, and thrive. If you’re with me, will you sign on to join me in renewing our commitment to continue Dr. King’s legacy — the work for justice and equality — in Michigan, today?](https://act.jocelynbenson.com/signup/voting_rights?refcode=AU_JMB_EG_GEN_PET_AC_20260119_PM1_V1_S1&t=2&akid=1386%2E181023%2EPjL7nk)**
[SIGN ON](https://act.jocelynbenson.com/signup/voting_rights?refcode=AU_JMB_EG_GEN_PET_AC_20260119_PM1_V1_S1&t=3&akid=1386%2E181023%2EPjL7nk)
---
Onward.
Jocelyn

* * *
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is a critical way we communicate with supporters like you.
* [Unsubscribe](https://act.jocelynbenson.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe-jbmi/?t=4&akid=1386%2E181023%2EPjL7nk)

PAID FOR BY JOCELYN BENSON FOR GOVERNOR

23133 Woodward Ave, Number 116,
Ferndale, MI 48220

Jerrod Sessler here.

I’m building a team of relentless, unapologetic patriots who will stand **shoulder to shoulder with President Trump** and**__fight like hell__ **to save this country.

name: A.G. Holder Alert
subject: A word from A.G. Holder on this MLK Day
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY THE NATIONAL REDISTRICTING ACTION FUND
body:
The fight to defend democracy is essential.
|
---

Peter, on this Martin Luther King Jr. Day, we are reflecting on the legacy of the civil rights movement that won historic protections of our rights.
It’s especially poignant at a time when our voting rights face an ongoing assault from anti-democracy extremists across the country and the U.S. Supreme Court is considering weakening the Voting Rights Act.
**As A.G. Holder put it, “we cannot shrink from the responsibility that rests, in this uniquely consequential moment, in our hands.”[Right now, we’re counting on your support to keep up the fight to defend democracy.](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123624469/596052281/742152064?refcode=don_aotl_tier1_email_HolderMLK_email_20260119-AOTL-Tier1-HolderMLK_&utm_source=email&utm_medium=email&utm_term=don&utm_content=20260119-AOTL-Tier1-HolderMLK_&utm_campaign=HolderMLK&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAxMy8xLzg5Mjk2IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogImRkYjc5OTQ5LTU4ZjUtZjAxMS04ZDRjLTAwMjI0ODJkMjc5YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=vdbVEhVVaMpDNu0h1hEa-pdYuu1zrzzC94pCKufgOGA=)**
[ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/123624470/596052282/742152064?refcode=don_aotl_tier1_email_HolderMLK_email_20260119-AOTL-Tier1-HolderMLK_&utm_source=email&utm_medium=email&utm_term=don&utm_content=20260119-AOTL-Tier1-HolderMLK_&utm_campaign=HolderMLK&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAxMy8xLzg5Mjk2IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogImRkYjc5OTQ5LTU4ZjUtZjAxMS04ZDRjLTAwMjI0ODJkMjc5YiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=vdbVEhVVaMpDNu0h1hEa-pdYuu1zrzzC94pCKufgOGA=)
---
The future of our most fundamental rights depends on what people like us do in moments like this.
Today and always, let’s channel Dr. King’s mission to uphold our civil rights and oppose the forces that seek to undermine the basic promise of a truly fair, truly representative democracy.
Thank you for strengthening our movement when it matters most,
The Redistricting Action Team

name: Rob Bonta
subject: How my dad came to march in Selma, Alabama
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY ROB BONTA FOR CALIFORNIA ATTORNEY GENERAL 2026 FPPC ID #145642
body:
https://secure.actblue.com/donate/rb-email-26?amount=25 [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/rb-email-26?amount=25]
Friends, as we honor the memory and legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., I want to take a moment to share how his work and message have shaped my life and my family.
My father, Warren, grew up surrounded by steep injustices in American society: Children denied education. Americans denied the right to vote. An entire race told they were less than, and treated as such, because of the color of their skin.
While he was studying at Berkeley, a friend mailed him some cassette tapes — recordings of a minister in the Deep South who was fighting these injustices.
He always said that the sermons were unlike anything he had heard before. He listened as a deep, resonant voice preached about a more just society — and everyday people changing the world.
That minister was Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., and those tapes compelled my dad to purchase a train ticket to the South to join his cause, marching for voting rights in Selma, Alabama.
As I grew up, my dad taught me the same lessons he learned from Dr. King, and from a lifetime spent fighting injustice.
He and my mom, who spent much of her life fighting for democracy and freedom in her home country of the Philippines, taught me that not only can everyday people change the world — we must.
In these tumultuous times, I've wondered how Dr. King would respond to today’s injustices if he were still alive.
In his "Letter from Birmingham City Jail," he wrote: “ We are caught in an inescapable network of mutuality, tied in a single garment of destiny. Whatever affects one directly, affects all indirectly. ”
Those words are just as relevant today as they were when he wrote them so eloquently decades ago. They apply to everyone, regardless of race, creed, gender, or religion.
Nearly 60 years after Dr. King’s death, our nation is so divided. We know that his dream is not yet realized.
Throughout my career in public service, I’m proud that we’ve taken steps to change our laws — to create more accountability in our institutions, to rid them of racial discrimination. But we know not all change comes from courts, or even from the legislature.
It is people — people lifting their voices in peaceful protest, speaking out against injustice, cruelty, and corruption — who drive us toward greater justice. Toward the society we need and deserve.
Across history, people power has always been the most potent and the most transformative power there is. That’s been true in the civil rights movement, in the farm worker movement, and in the restoration of democracy in countries around the world. It will be true today, too.
Dr. King taught us to use our people power to fight boldly for equality and dignity for all — and that righteous fight is one we must continue, together.
He believed that progress is not inevitable: it is built by everyday people, guided by conscience and willing to act.
That belief shaped my family. It has shaped my life in public service. And it must continue to shape our country.
On this day, and in the work ahead, may we honor Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., not only in memory, but in action — choosing justice, humanity, and our shared responsibility to one another.
Sincerely,
Rob Bonta
CONTRIBUTE [https://secure.actblue.com/donate/rb-email-26?amount=25]
To contribute via check, please address to:
Rob Bonta for Attorney General 2026
c/o Deane & Company
1700 Tribute Road, Suite 201
Sacramento, CA 95815
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com . Thank you for supporting Rob Bonta. Grassroots supporters like you help us protect California’s working families, advance criminal justice reform, and keep our communities safe. Emails are a crucial way to stay in touch with our top supporters. If we're going to protect and serve the people of California, we need you with us every step of the way.
If you'd still like to unsubscribe from our emails, click unsubscribe: https://secure.ngpvan.com/p/tsXL_NR2qEmgRRek5GtgsA2?unsubscribedata=nmVfw5DZeQbicUGH5zrDR8SYxZ%2FMrK16YzkzbN8GEXBcA8St%2Fg9l1GUI5IgaeuoSVtaGji9KP3UMKd%2BOM%2FADY%2FY75eKqkYnF8foq98RkYC19eZqJyiV%2B8jv%2BHytVTZ3h9%2FFC1QhmnGkbsW7SUY88aFaqTk1F3sdQZjqfTrYo7vd3vu6zuukIG9ohK2fOgus7o61mlsr7X0oTOmmI1QSeH8yxP0baJCOVlSipLEJq4iw8UCti1%2BYUZHc3PLRzCSBxn6553OS%2FajRVZfPHzVLPWscj%2BfXURp%2B3GsA6IhaJS1DMMo10NpA7gxhO7pXIfTfEpzYJWLclhRA1AUTGzYBr989RXafestxyUJw6tQDiXFzSDKXYeHhhnLQtCZCfwcwSy%2BMBDQDbVwDRakUp6Lu6oFjqoUMmGGOUesCFIGJehIjMynFf3eJK5XCj56mja8TR .
From all of us at Team Bonta, thank you for your support!

Throughout my career in public service, I’m proud that we’ve taken steps to change our laws — to create more accountability in our institutions, to rid them of racial discrimination. But we know not all change comes from courts, or even from the legislature. It is people — people lifting their voices in peaceful protest, speaking out against injustice, cruelty, and corruption — who drive us toward greater justice. Toward the society we need and deserve. Across history, people power has always been the most potent and the most transformative power there is. That’s been true in the civil rights movement, in the farm worker movement, and in the restoration of democracy in countries around the world. It will be true today, too. Dr. King taught us to use our people power to fight boldly for equality and dignity for all — and that righteous fight is one we must continue, together. He believed that progress is not inevitable: it is built by everyday people, guided by conscience and willing to act. That belief shaped my family. It has shaped my life in public service. And it must continue to shape our country. On this day, and in the work ahead, may we honor Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., not only in memory, but in action — choosing justice, humanity, and our shared responsibility to one another. Sincerely, Rob Bonta CONTRIBUTE [] To contribute via check, please address to: Rob Bonta for Attorney General 2026 c/o Deane & Company 1700 Tribute Road, Suite 201 Sacramento, CA 95815

# [](https://act.betoorourke.com/go/43706?t=1&akid=240614%2E444211%2EKMqnJV)
We have a rare chance right now to do something big in Texas.
Senate District 9—a place Republicans thought was untouchable—is in play. Democrat Taylor Rehmet forced a runoff after beating two Republicans in a district Trump won by _17 points_. That means for the first time in years, people power (not money or incumbency or corporations) can decide the outcome.
Winning in SD9 would show that Democrats can compete and win even in the toughest parts of this country. It sets the tone for November and reminds the state what’s possible when we organize, talk to neighbors, and register and turn out new voters.
But the reality is this runoff is razor-thin. Every conversation, every volunteer shift, every voter reached matters. Taylor got 48% the first time meaning the next 3% will tip the balance. That’s why we need to act now.
**We’re on the ground, training volunteers, educating voters, and activating the young and first-time voters who often decide these close races. But we can’t do it without you. Your support fuels the organizing that moves the needle.**

name: Demand Justice PAC
subject: It’s capitulation, and it’s unacceptable
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Demand Justice PAC
body:
[ https://act.demandjustice.org/go/54940?refcode=em20260119-61096&t=1001&akid=61096%2E1407376%2E3XzUXh ]Demand Justice PAC[ https://act.demandjustice.org/go/54940?refcode=em20260119-61096&t=1002&akid=61096%2E1407376%2E3XzUXh ]Demand Justice PAC

February judicial hearings are coming fast — and history has shown that
rather than take a stand against Trump, Senate Democrats would rather fold
and advance his crony judges.

Take a look about what folks have been saying:

[ https://act.demandjustice.org/go/55459?c0=dj-em-20260119.RR4zak&refcode=em20260119-61096&amounts=5%2C20%2C50%2C100%2C250%2C500%2C1000&amount=5&t=1003&akid=61096%2E1407376%2E3XzUXh ]Comments from people about Senate Democrats advancing Trump's crony
judges.

These nominees are Trump loyalists. They refuse to tell the truth about
the 2020 election. They dodge basic questions about January 6. They won’t
even acknowledge an insurrection — all so they don’t upset Donald Trump.

And yet, 18 Senate Democrats keep voting to confirm them anyway.

It’s capitulation, and it’s unacceptable.

Trump has been explicit about his goal: stack the courts with judges who
will protect him, enable authoritarianism, and bend the rule of law to
serve one man. Every confirmation hearing is a chance to stop that power
grab — and every Democratic “yes” vote helps him pull it off.

Demand Justice is not waiting quietly for this to play out.

We’re bringing the pressure now — before these hearings happen — to force
Senate Democrats to oppose Trump’s nominees and actually act like the
firewall they promised to be. That means public pressure, accountability
campaigns, and making sure every Judiciary Democrat knows the country is
watching.

But we can only do that with the strength of this grassroots team behind
us.

[ https://act.demandjustice.org/go/55459?c0=dj-em-20260119.RR4zak&refcode=em20260119-61096&amounts=5%2C20%2C50%2C100%2C250%2C500%2C1000&amount=5&t=1004&akid=61096%2E1407376%2E3XzUXh ]If you’re ready to help us turn up the heat before February hearings

If any member of the Senate Judiciary Committee helps advance these
nominees, we will call them out by name, expose their hypocrisy, and make
sure voters know exactly who chose Trump’s judges over democracy.

In solidarity,

Demand Justice PAC

| | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

clean_body: The political world is in shock. A seemingly minor misstep by President Trump has set off a chain reaction that’s shaking Washington to its core. | | | Don't want to receive our publications? | | | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | | | | The political world is in shock. | A seemingly minor has set off a chain reaction that’s shaking Washington to its core. Behind closed doors, lawmakers are whispering about impeachment ... while powerful insiders scramble to contain the fallout. What started as a small oversight could now — and the future of America as we know it. Some say this could be the turning point of a generation. Others say it’s already too late. — before it disappears from the headlines. This ad is sent on behalf of American Alternative Assets at 21550 W Oxnard St Ste 845, Woodland Hills, California 91367. # M.L.K. DAY 2026
---
| |
---
| | My friend, As we observe Martin Luther King Jr. Day, I want to take a moment to reflect on how far our great nation has come. From the dark days of slavery, through the injustices of Jim Crow, and the turbulent 1960s, America has made tremendous progress toward fulfilling Dr. King's vision of a society where people are judged by the content of their character, not the color of their skin. Dr. King’s dream was rooted in equality, opportunity, and justice for all, principles that Republicans have long championed. Today, we continue that fight by standing for policies that lift everyone: safer communities, better schools, economic freedom, and opportunity without government overreach or division based on race. In the 12th District and across our country, Republicans are working every day to keep America focused on character, merit, hard work, and unity, not identity politics or division. We honor Dr. King’s legacy by ensuring his message endures: that every American, regardless of background, deserves a fair shot at the American Dream. Let’s carry forward his call for justice and equality as we build a stronger, more united NC-12. Thank you for your continued support and commitment. Yours in service, Addul Ali
Candidate for U.S. Congress

DAY 2026 | | | | My friend, As we observe Martin Luther King Jr. Day, I want to take a moment to reflect on how far our great nation has come. From the dark days of slavery, through the injustices of Jim Crow, and the turbulent 1960s, America has made tremendous progress toward fulfilling Dr. King's vision of a society where people are judged by the content of their character, not the color of their skin. Dr. King’s dream was rooted in equality, opportunity, and justice for all, principles that Republicans have long championed. Today, we continue that fight by standing for policies that lift everyone: safer communities, better schools, economic freedom, and opportunity without government overreach or division based on race. In the 12th District and across our country, Republicans are working every day to keep America focused on character, merit, hard work, and unity, not identity politics or division. We honor Dr. King’s legacy by ensuring his message endures: that every American, regardless of background, deserves a fair shot at the American Dream. Let’s carry forward his call for justice and equality as we build a stronger, more united NC-12. Thank you for your continued support and commitment. Yours in service, Addul Ali Candidate for U.S. Congress North Carolina’s 12th DistrictPH:704-750-5057 Sponsor the campaign here : | | | | | | | UPCOMING EVENTS | | | | | | | | | | | This email was sent from .

name: Sarah McBride
subject: Honoring the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr
disclaimer_text: Paid for by McBride for Delaware
body:
#
In moments of darkness and uncertainty, it can be tempting to believe that hope is a luxury we can’t afford. That it’s something we return to only when times are easy. But the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. teaches us something very different: hope is essential. Hope is enduring. And most importantly, hope is a practice. One that we must commit to over, and over again.
**_“We stand in life at midnight; we are always at the threshold of a new dawn.”_
– Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.**
Many people across Delaware and our nation are feeling the weight of midnight right now. The challenges before us are pressing, and the path forward isn’t always clear. But Dr. King reminds us that midnight is not the conclusion of our story. He understood that these moments of struggle are not permanent, and that even in our most difficult hours, the possibility of renewal is never out of reach.
Midnight, instead, is a moment of decision. A moment when we choose whether to retreat in fear, or to step forward together with purpose, courage, and resolve. And when we choose service over indifference, courage over cynicism, and hope over despair, we help bring that new dawn closer into view.
I believe deeply that both individually and collectively, we possess the capacity to meet this moment. I believe it because I’ve seen it firsthand: in the willingness to stay engaged even when progress stalls, in the steady work of finding common ground, and in the everyday acts of leadership and kindness that quietly move us forward.
As we honor Dr. King’s life and legacy today, we are called not only to reflect, but to act. Let us turn our shared values into tangible support for our neighbors and communities, and carry forward Dr. King’s vision through service and civic engagement.
Thank you for your commitment to our state, your care for your neighbors, and your unwavering belief in what we can accomplish together.
With gratitude and determination,
– Sarah McBride

From day one, Donald Trump has used the power of the presidency to push through disastrous policies that do nothing to address the issues that so many people are facing.

He’s pardoned January 6th cop beaters and rioters. He’s imposed reckless tariffs that have made life even more expensive. He’s gutted and privatized the VA, putting my fellow veterans’ earned care at risk. He’s slashed funding for Medicaid and SNAP, while handing out tax giveaways to billionaires and corporate bosses.

**And he’s repeatedly attacked our democracy, ignored the Constitution, and threatened those who disagreed with him— _even calling for my death after I restated a basic truth about our Constitution._**

Derek, the President is unhinged. And instead of standing up to Trump and fighting for their constituents, many Congressional Republicans are acting like his lapdogs.

Unlike those politicians, I refuse to cower to the President. I’m fighting back to restore the American Dream, put power back in the hands of hardworking people, protect the promises of our Constitution, and make life less of a ripoff.

name: Adam Schwarze, former Navy SEAL
subject: A regular guy seeking a big job
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Schwarze for Senate
body:
You don’t know me, but you know what men like me stand for and our reputation |

---

Fellow Conservative,

My name is Adam Schwarze, and I’m a former Navy SEAL, Marine Corps veteran, and proud Minnesotan **running for U.S. Senate against Tim Walz’s handpicked lieutenant governor—a politician even more radical than her boss.** I’m requesting your support in this critical battle to restore common-sense conservative values to Minnesota.

I hold a Master’s from Harvard, a Master’s Certificate from Johns Hopkins in Security and Nuclear Deterrence, and an MBA. But my proudest achievement is serving our country to make my parents, especially my mom, proud. I want to serve you in the U.S. Senate, fighting for the values that have made Minnesota and America great for generations.

[](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GBwT4lb3yGklSuxCrLU5lbkCRBgGCy0UNmmgRPY-GX1TpT5lo5Chedpu5vikpgfUAWu_hnc99UfaYC7wLa9Aooqs1bnwlUUPBYBt_ZaMmj6PgrKcepyqPcoQloL6xekSgROHTd-r6V0lHBQX0wyJawq20rTbrRLbJeNsMhC_7hUaU_eYFxHtXunvvOxvvvo0DFJsUxMFbDuTmOBK7p_uFCQcRieRQX7VTjjYhxzc0ojzU1t2_QgLpyf6JEOkLTOESdGf1YNOSpZaUI2XqxGFMtpLaLEoFHrE3T00zkiK01K_XV45z1uVsQpD6ElZWica7eXUInku822IhbMHgKjMoRa9D2MxX242DUD7wpK-onZVT-n0YtzRig8S-lEI_d-2zry9IjVZVgrb5B0yla_y-QUSuczVm-FUtrSJx-gVmsAC1mce5Th9xn5xuFGWGKT8hS6Emx13ftDgmib_yay4H0taENpQCnCVaUFE1G2gERI_osijZFKs45pXdzxN8oOeIUA%3D%3D)

**I’m starting this race with absolutely nothing** , but because of the enormity of the race's outcome, I figured there is no harm in asking for your help. At the end of the day, my request of you will end in one of two ways:
* 1.) **[you contribute to help elect a former Navy SEAL to the United States Senate](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GB-XQhBixRaLM9VQnGE6wDOW493O3bsQMCXz84TWSCVgdUB_rcDSIxJNeZw28K7vE71AXPbeZuFG_9d5jGhb5zvcHHj4MzIwbEdYfjegPeNWhnTrjq3R3lroR2px78T8xIiwCpTQIiJGB7GYsaYfQyXq1AJOW9bFIOXlqi57LDDca8jplMPpNFmdWZsXopWoOVpqAZXa-FKsuylh-CJKrHPIWvA1vsZrBgr3Wj5DKRzyUTH9EfLRhQAtuaHKQ4MKKf-T-fnrNubTLANn3qmTBYd0Vsms-bLyhnlAhXSGC1OPWET0V9P-rYDW9NwHzztR7CjFmi5d8tXSWvBevxdzJkQdeAlSBNmtdG53-4-7KSsera5QkCxYDpMFUamKAZwF-S6dKoVbgyr4FxWiRErlTUOX2ZD6I_EbRMlzeoI96dWQ7QtV1TNyZc2NUxRoEM55kQpaNjJi_OWF02NXQfsdbWsgItVFy2UMir5M-rlOxjAuimTNe-lok394go6LxK1o-Yg%3D%3D)**.
* 2.) you read this email without contributing, but you at least now know who I am.

[**HELP ME GET MY CAMPAIGN OFF THE GROUND
CONTRIBUTE $2 OR MORE **](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GBxO7UOly6wJTOZVwAuRuqScfw4ol5NP5SaTrmfJIs7_XL4yUXkbqRBag4ShohEaZWCimzRF7b-P1SiAAnszDxWLynqtvUFBh9Cn3Phq9w0Vrpbbm7KM5YcRW6n_IzO0QJnqf2HBSPzEK3JWCHx-ngpzdg22kbyjQGxq2Mw3Es1E_cjkfsuUg9IpPF-553j6O3aUtSZDQ09bSi_tlDGe7ldBMxPY21XsNXlREJDr9g8C-1kyXq9qBzo--3RxUBXSryU3pL4r53mGGJaVv5cxso4XZNduhVnr92irFht31mWwCBK9NWP4NU0iffKuqmu-FG_YuaYHFBdhQGGZDE2bsJxOOeW7GhotjKv8rGEJ8MLSjeGxObuOE0SsZfh-u70vUSSz0h1mdwG0S7FRQKR9fKKByM1MDSHwj9ewVTMr-JhQm7MTs-W4s_nSoVfsCMaxoumOvd-dri-rIuQn3BiRHnAzVz-gYFek7VlqwUFueA8DHQ5PL43NVtMLE1pYbhpSd1Q%3D%3D)
**I’m not a career politician. I’m a regular guy from a working-class family who grew up playing hockey and answered our nation’s call on my 18th birthday in October 2001, just weeks after the 9/11 attacks.** I joined the Marine Corps to defend our country, and as a Navy SEAL, I deployed nine times to over 70 countries, risking my life to protect our freedoms and way of life. Unlike Tim Walz, who retired from the National Guard before his unit deployed to Iraq, I never backed down from serving our nation during war.

Minnesota is at a crossroads. Under Governor Walz and his lieutenant governor, our state has been dragged left by radical policies that fueled the violent unrest in Minneapolis in 2020, **defunded our police** , and burdened hardworking families with higher taxes and inflation. **While our streets burned, the lieutenant governor pushed lawsuits against law enforcement, undermining the officers keeping us safe.** This isn’t the Minnesota I grew up in—or the one any Minnesotan deserves.

CONTRIBUTE $2 OR MORE**](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GByVwoAghJpskGWzko7ACmTB91A5HHZOWaE1IVlW1A969igFOTUA_8bSJS2xx1r3v8UsOXqje88sOekLDYOSIfgLdynGQId1u7P7F-lVE5MLIvTFoCjnCEVzDbG_npdsuNmPmpn2UibUViu-j685GTyWKz_b1pOnDsG5tmohmTtZiRqJ4oiRJBJqAEKqx9nQFvhWimZfEgpWI8FVfaWNEiFF8e59yYbV2bv4DX3NtSmj7TuDsl1IYlM6N5lNVZ9zgttMTBwK9zKLpX4tt0vGztVzp7yygBiDa2c3aq_pfBPQr70LyoaTeigmorx4yfWju72tIJEkLU7hPpdbakDh-3c4AGh_OVx5b23zk4LnkQ9j-AI7-Qdt3LZuFTuM4c0tFEXpmfhhjm-6v2xPjYkuVCxkQBJXnlZ8tglDleF_Lshh-GhJ5LrUvQ_h0swRUxFWIUVv9Z6j2NjLHEkx49CeQAT3caPRThJZbzt4VvDNzYD7527YqJcDtv3YIavkbjM1WFA%3D%3D)

**If you are reading this email right now and you don’t live in Minnesota, then I’m sure you’ll agree with me that every American must be represented by individuals in Congress who want to build a better version of the America we live in today—individuals of integrity.**

_**That’s not who my opponent is. **_

We have an incredible opportunity to elect a Republican to the U.S. Senate next year - something voters in this state haven’t done since they re-elected former Senator Norm Coleman in 2002. As a Republican, I know the odds are tough in a state that hasn’t voted red in a presidential election since 1976. But **as a former Navy SEAL, I thrive in tough fights. I’ve faced worse odds overseas and am ready to bring that same grit to Washington.**

[](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GByXNz04vGGzzUcy7wab6bbSU4F-KuLWRLmz-sEqfHRYMa4TpySZLTgCMEGgN2BFjZyGFtjzmgEAxhQbNVlOE6u7C4C1ceafIC8fYZCIi3ll97dDs8R5o6rrbkIOhGJ_121iWDA0-cYO6dlfrncJLWX4gBsFyFGnhfwZC0o11RtvvMtu6lMfNxbtZrqc6rtoZiFPZxYMbXMBYAKubksRyviy6PnUGFSYp7KUD1fDbsB-sE3ucxu1Gh4dJ3RJZMgYSKLCLUNpjoF1ee8BNy2b5O-JG8gN2uIsJ8GqfGgGlXJHzlZNgR_RI-LFsHaWRFkiQGZIg4_9CFny4pd2MIKqu7IBge4rH6GqzjDh8JH9EMgT9kz7-3NVxkAmao9nVbDiqpdbENCW0btnxPxCFSRFjlnl6Kau1spF3n3KEwq8zYhAz3reRjXSEQDnGab-28UvdZ-iKfJTdVQeAPO5piwNioRuc_QOXbLbi48LjVq21IeunWWW-JLbs2c9j0g9Y-QGwXQ%3D%3D)

CONTRIBUTE $2 OR MORE**](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GBz31e9ic5O2Vio9OYAZTGPydw4Am-A00OyPrPxb7tlVDc3zDc_FTGk-3VrC1XsxbUlhEPxkcWuXMBCq_UoV_BNmeggKRAVd_mtxploef7imN2M2LE8fhciCxydV4HLC3L4DAD993wsxJb6BalNIUmCQ-ecMuA9vpavmfPMlvn7iE6xgwQiTXMGJ4i-MMzXCkUNWFO40ui3pbOS_u0yyQ3_MEfMKagR76eUH5_S9FSYzYfDOjVtjOs-p0dirBC-scQpN2V8pDLJOnev2E3sU5raME0EXms7uMoqCkvnlENgGSUvqDRPlvR_QmfE-dTMgX7CJddpi4l4GOs1unPsg6fOg1OB4kEtxaS1OvxpBrvR4CqpfmjYIB1qHHciux6rRed_3_X8-Bq6CCII9EKu5Tkhb0jOccswcHo4QUS7rMBjj00nDxK9oA8ZTv-cKXa9W6sSwMX7i_rWPdKTFYjwEfKQbIBVIAPuEPe7PE2d3STbAtKo2axG8egIX8M1NO_9R0JA%3D%3D)
Here’s what I stand for:
* Restoring law and order by supporting our police and cracking down on crime.
* Cutting taxes and regulations to ease the burden on retirees and small businesses.
* Securing our borders to protect our communities and sovereignty.
* Defending our Second Amendment rights and traditional values.
* Stopping the radical left’s agenda that’s eroding the America we love.

You don’t know me, but you know what men like me stand for and our reputation. So, **[I’m asking you to take a chance on me and support my campaign today, in its infancy, to help me get it off the ground and give us a shot at reclaiming a Senate seat we haven’t held in a generation](http://links.ourdefenseofamerica.com/u/click?_t=aa3ef0db03454a26b650c9ca7e919386&_m=0da75008eab24464962f7490e076e51c&_e=ZIU71w0_Oyvhso8yQI1L3Fj12HW7DDEGgXEco2KNA2VUVsdfKgmBapCvDknjTDOCB9ph2KsnG2cfiK05T6Ur-11PW_MrWm1CgDOdxq-eJoBSshnNLD0gMINdrTEr9O_7f79_5__jIUTnYpqDEIyffma2Ze5E0RdbHSE3fGMzFaElYBnjU90aQ2UZXkav-ulRzao7SR0UpCYzz6J3GS7zBxmE97h5HM6HzfcGJWq_Za_3XzWSCOo_bRCBQEtvyemvRzqeXFfWymArM_Igtf3GB48W-ufCX7pqkbqeJat5evNZK3jN-BkKyHt6gm7A_kKkX5AyRt2T2cT82L0jffA1rwA7Q7n53XxrpioH5sMLmrXQrx0kC-fGDoJm73RUKyeen8KTPEAPbPB320s-FuCEnQsgad00gIBFRJUlkffGJEDICQExLdUAjabMelo_wxxeV-R__uuiG7Zh2mv2DmRhpdoPYehTwIzCNIBsMof4XQpvZ41ns6w8GDfHy9Zfdamzb2uAfymaar3zfreG1Y0CGH-KWNjDuMb_cb7OZJnx69Tqt2WcxB0T8nu67sywfXvSIk9XcPKjPBjvhgJvN9PZGO0VJf3ZOZxYOvhEKO1wI-p__IA9XbsS3YCMLzcSFUuuvl3_Fksc5Y6Rx_9mMLCu-cQMjMwCIE86F257SoTPlFg1HKgHgZLZcQ2RHUvqFGmw3HJx7BZP2oIAe59oD2k-2m5s70-kePoXT7l8SorXkgkC77XVpYlIbQfbbfBkfbavuJM1eSZ5r6OyP4_i6y1q7g%3D%3D)**.
